other_side: Zitron's answer to the build-a-learning-loop gospel is blunt: you cannot build durable value on something whose return you cannot measure. He argues nobody can calculate the ROI of AI — or even a standard cost per task — because hallucination-prone models and an ever-growing list of harnesses make every interaction unpredictable. The fashionable talk of owning "token capital", on his telling, rests on subsidised and deliberately obfuscated token prices; the moment Anthropic and OpenAI moved enterprises to usage-based billing in early 2026, customers revolted and began capping spend. Far from compounding into owned IP, he insists, AI keeps getting more expensive, not cheaper, and much of the enthusiasm is written by people "conned into ignoring the true cost."
truth_beneath: Both men orbit one variable: whether a company can wring measurable, durable value from AI that exceeds its true, unsubsidised cost. Both accept the same facts — token prices were subsidised and obfuscated, and enterprises recoiled once moved to usage-based billing. The empirical question that resolves it is whether the private evals, captured workflows and feedback loops Nadella prescribes actually raise measured output by more than the real per-token bill; the observable is audited enterprise AI ROI, net renewal and spend over coming quarters, and whether such "token capital" survives a model swap. The residue is pure worldview: Whittemore sees a compounding asset a firm can own, Zitron sees unmeasurable, subsidised theatre. Whittemore is vindicated if firms show durable, portable gains net of rising prices; Zitron if spend keeps being capped with no measurable return. Both are polemicists with incentives — Whittemore bullish and anti-doom, Zitron bubble-certain and anti-hype, each with a newsletter to sell — so the signal is their shared premise that unit economics, not model choice, now decides. Watch whether any large firm publishes audited AI ROI net of true token costs.
